titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 12 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender writes a frustrated letter concerning the lack of a definitive answer to his request for leave. The letter appears to have been written on the back of a set of instructions for soldiers on leave.
Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/217

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
12. April My pv A perfectly rampant
bw as I have not yet got
a decision as to my going
is whether I start tomorrow
night or later. All I know
is that the General is quite
willing for me to go any-
time I can be spared.
I may turn up Friday
I hope fairly early as this
boat is supposed to leave
Boulougne at 6.15AM. It
will be so so my inquiry
so either I or a most assured
come letter in my place
should reach you. Your bw
A. F. W. 3337
Instructions for Officers
and Men proceeding on Leave. 1. Rifles will be taken but no ammuni—
tion. This is to be left with unit. 2. Combined warrants and tickets to
destination and returns to be obtained from
unit before departure. The Station of
destination not merely the country must
be inserted on the warrant. 3. Men will be collected into parties in
such formations as may be ordered at
Railheads under officers detailed for the
duty. The senior Officer will be in command
of the party and be responsible that the
men entrain together and are marched on
board the ship and keep together whilst on
board and are marched off and entrained
together at Port of arrival, and the same
procedure will be adopted for the return
journey, the same officer collecting his men
together at Victoria Station or Waterloo.
The men will be expected to assist by
rejoining their proper parties as quickly as
possible. 4. Pay to be obtained before leaving the
unit. French money will be exchanged
at Victoria Station and Waterloo NOT at
Port of departure. 5. A copy of these rules will be given to
each man with his leave form. BSD — B. M229/64/11. 11/15.
